justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6, plus a bonus BR-7). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1, record visibility BR-5, note lock BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Scheduling invariant (BR-2) marked invariant: true. Cancellation rules (BR-3, BR-4) correctly capture the role boundary between patient and admin, with BR-3/BR-4 marked invariant: false as operational policy. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1, AC-2), double-booking attempt (AC-3, AC-12), late cancellation attempt (AC-5, AC-6), and unauthorized record access (AC-8). Confidence level of 78 is within the 65-90 range, with clearly stated assumptions about license validation, treating relationship definition, and interval semantics.

observations.text: Invariant classifications are precise and well-justified — each invariant_justification explains why the rule is or isn't a true invariant using the 'corrupt state' test
BR-3 and BR-4 correctly split the cancellation rule into patient vs admin roles, capturing the role boundary the rubric requires
12 acceptance criteria is exceptional coverage — includes concurrent booking (AC-12), boundary conditions, and both positive and negative test cases
Edge cases are thorough and practical — boundary inclusivity, license revocation, back-to-back appointments with half-open intervals, concurrent note modification near lock deadline
Escalations identify genuine ambiguities with concrete options and recommendations, showing domain reasoning beyond surface-level rule extraction
Confidence assumptions explicitly address what 'licensed' means in the domain model, as required by the rubric
Bonus BR-7 (note editing within the 48h window) complements BR-6 by making the editable-vs-locked lifecycle explicit
